elements.sent-2411-001: I model a rational agent who spends resources between the current time and some fixed future deadline.
elements.sent-2411-002: Opportunities to spend resources arise randomly according to a Poisson process, and the quality of each opportunity follows a uniform distribution.
elements.sent-2411-003: The agent values their current resource stock at exactly the sum of expected utility from all future spending opportunities.
elements.sent-2411-004: Unlike in traditional discounted expected utility models, the agent exhibits correlation aversion, static (but not dynamic) preference reversals, and monotonicity with respect to payment timing.
elements.sent-2411-005: Connecting the agent's risk and time preference is intuitive, and doing so leads to a new model of procrastination where the agent misperceives their general attitude toward spending resources.

elements.sent-2411-008: On a personal level, readers of this paper will be familiar with deadline pressure in any number of situations.
elements.sent-2411-009: My goal in writing this paper is to investigate how deadlines affect incentives, and I model a single rational agent who moves forward through time until they reach some fixed, known deadline $T$.
elements.sent-2411-010: I solve the problem how should the agent optimally spend resources when the timing and quality of allocation opportunities are uncertain.
elements.sent-2411-011: I take a broad view of what resources, spending, and quality mean in this context.
elements.sent-2411-012: If we model a budget, the agent literally spends money on goods, and higher quality means that a store or website has more items in stock when the agent visits.
elements.sent-2411-013: Perhaps the agent needs to eat several food items before they spoil, and quality of a spending opportunity represents how hungry the agent is in that moment.
elements.sent-2411-014: In all cases, quality can encompass the agent's mood or other subjective feeling toward the task at hand.
elements.sent-2411-015: We imagine the agent has a resource stock $x$, and at any given time $t$, they encounter an opportunity to spend resources with probability $\lambda dt$.
elements.sent-2411-016: Formally, spending opportunities follow a Poisson process with rate parameter $\lambda$ on the interval $(-\infty,T]$, and past time $T$, the agent never encounters another opportunity.
elements.sent-2411-017: Resources are valuable to the agent only to the extent that the agent can spend them, so the valuation for the resource stock comes from possible future opportunities and is null from $T$ onward.
elements.sent-2411-018: Whenever the agent reaches a spending opportunity, they first learn the quality $\theta$ associated with the opportunity, which is uniform on $[0,1]$ and independent of other opportunities.
elements.sent-2411-019: Then the agent chooses $y$ resources to save, spends the remaining $x-y$ portion, and derives utility equal to $u(\theta,x-y)$, where $u$ is the agent's instantaneous utility function.
elements.sent-2411-020: Moving forward in time, the agent faces the same problem except with $y$ resources instead of $x$, so the agent's valuation of their resource stock solves a dynamic programming problem with $x$ and $t$ as the state variables.
elements.sent-2411-021: To avoid any confounding effects and to keep the model as simple as possible, I assume the agent simply adds together instantaneous utility from spending opportunities with no inherent time preference.
elements.sent-2411-022: The assumptions of the previous paragraph lead to a solution with rich properties that can inform our understanding of deadlines and delay.
elements.sent-2411-023: A solution to the model is a saving rule $y(\theta, t,x)$ that means the following: if the agent has $x$ resources at time $t$, and a spending opportunity arises with quality $\theta$, the agent saves $y(\theta, t,x)$ resources and spends $x-y(\theta, t,x)$.
elements.sent-2411-024: We abreviate the spending rule to $y$ when the arguments are clear from context.
elements.sent-2411-025: Dual to $y$ is the agent's value function $V(t,x)$, which captures the expected sum of instantaneous utility from all future spending opportunities if the agent has $x$ resources at time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-026: Finding $y$ is equivalent to finding $V$, but as is typically the case in dynamic programming problems, $V$ is more tractable and will occupy more of our focus in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-027: As an example of solution properties, $V$ is decreasing in $t$, increasing in $x$, and concave in both arguments with negative cross-partial derivative.
elements.sent-2411-028: Properties of $V$ translate to properties of $y$, and for example, with all else being equal, the agent spends more at the next opportunity when it arrives later.

elements.sent-2411-108: In evaluating homogeneity with respect to time, the decision theory literature considers three properties of time preference: stationarity, time invariance, and time consistency.
elements.sent-2411-109: We can most easily understand these properties in terms of a reference period and an evaluation period.
elements.sent-2411-110: An agent's current time is their reference period, and at the evaluation period, the agent chooses between two alternatives.
elements.sent-2411-111: Stationarity means the agent's choice is independent of the evaluation period, and time invariance means that changing both the reference period and the evaluation period in the same way does not affect the agent's choice.
elements.sent-2411-112: Time consistency means the agent's chooses an alternative independently of the reference period.
elements.sent-2411-113: See \textcite{halevy-2015 for discussion of these properties.}
elements.sent-2411-114: I am less clear on the origins of time consistency, but the concept dates back at least to \textcite{samuelson-1937 .}
elements.sent-2411-115: These properties affect the agent's behavior by prohibiting preference reversals.
elements.sent-2411-116: A preference reversal means that an agent previously prefered one option, but moving somehow forward in time prompted the agent to switch their choice.
elements.sent-2411-117: A static preference reversal means that changing the evaluation period (while fixing the reference period) caused the switch, and a dynamic preference reversal is the opposite.
elements.sent-2411-118: Dynamic preference reversals have received more attention than static preference reversals, to the point that some authors omit the word ``dynamic'' and use the general term ``preference reversal'' to refer to what I call dynamic preference reversal.
elements.sent-2411-119: An agent with stationary time preferences never exhibits static preference reversal, and an agent with time consistent time preferences never exhibits dynamic preference reversal.
elements.sent-2411-120: Time invariance makes both types of preference reversals equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-121: See \textcite{chen-et-al-2019 for more discussion of static versus dynamic preference reversals.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Because I study deadlines, my model by definition violates time invariance, and I show later in this paper that my model also violates stationarity.
elements.sent-2411-123: Because the agent's value function comes from a Bellman equation, the agent exhibits time consistency.
elements.sent-2411-124: If the agent of this paper chooses between a smaller-sooner or later-larger payment, the agent prefers the larger payment away from the deadline and the smaller payment near the deadline.
elements.sent-2411-125: Far away from the deadline, the agent recognizes that they will eventually switch to a smaller-sooner payment, so the preference reversal is static.
elements.sent-2411-126: The preference reversal is not dynamic because it happens in a time consistent fashion.
elements.sent-2411-127: Once the agent reaches the time period when they expect to switch to a smaller payment, they actually do so.

elements.sent-2411-134: We consider a rational agent with a resource stock $x$.
elements.sent-2411-135: The agent spends their resources at various points in time and derives utility according to their instantaneous utility function $u$.
elements.sent-2411-136: By definition, $u$ takes two arguments, one for the quality $\theta$ of the spending opportunity and one for the amount $x$ of resources spent.
elements.sent-2411-137: We assume that $u(0,x)=u(\theta,0)=0$ and that $u$ is smooth and strictly increasing in $x$ and $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-138: We also assume that $u$ is separable, so we can write $u(\theta,x)=\zeta(\theta)\mu(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-139: This technical assumption means that the marginal effects of $\theta$ and $x$ on $u$ are independent of the other variable.

elements.sent-2411-452: In this paper, I investigate how deadlines affect incentives in the presence of randomness, and I model deadline pressure and procrastination.
elements.sent-2411-453: I consider a single rational agent who possesses a resource stock and moves forward through time until some fixed, known deadline $T$.
elements.sent-2411-454: The agent can consume resources only at specific spending opportunities, and the timing and quality of each opportunity are random and exogenous.
elements.sent-2411-455: For example, consider a funding agency that solicits proposals and distributes awards on a rolling basis until some internal deadline.
elements.sent-2411-456: The agency's budget is a resource stock, and each proposal is a spending opportunity.
elements.sent-2411-457: Whenever the agency receives a new proposal, it decides how much of its budget to allocate toward the proposal based on how well it aligns with the agency's aims.
elements.sent-2411-458: From the agency's perspective, proposals come in at random, and the funding agency does not know exactly how many it will receive before the deadline.
elements.sent-2411-459: Both the agent from my model and the funding agency of our example need to account for this uncertainty in spending opportunities if they want to consume resources optimally.
elements.sent-2411-460: This situation induces deadline pressure because the rational agent and the funding agency are compensating for the possibility that a given spending opportunity may be the last one.
elements.sent-2411-461: In mathematical symbols, the agent's resource stock is $x$, and at any given time $t$, they encounter an opportunity to spend resources with probability $\lambda dt$.
elements.sent-2411-462: Formally, spending opportunities follow a Poisson process with rate parameter $\lambda$ on the interval $(-\infty,T]$, and after time $T$, the agent cannot spend more resources.
elements.sent-2411-463: In our example, $x$ is the size of the agency's budget, and $\lambda$ controls how quickly proposals arrive.
elements.sent-2411-464: If the agency is well-known and has an easy application process, we expect $\lambda$ to be relatively large, and if not, $\lambda$ may be smaller.
elements.sent-2411-465: The funding agency's budget expires at the deadline, so $x$ exogenously becomes 0 at $T$ in that case.
elements.sent-2411-466: If the deadline is public rather than internal, people will not submit proposals after $T$, and $\lambda$ also exogenously becomes 0.
elements.sent-2411-467: What's important here is that resources are useful only to the extent that the agent can spend them, so the valuation for the resource stock comes from possible future opportunities and is null from $T$ onward.
elements.sent-2411-468: In applications, we should expect at least one of $x$ or $\lambda$ to become 0 at $T$, but for the math in this paper, we remain agnostic about how the deadline manifests in regard to the agent's decision problem.
elements.sent-2411-469: When our hypothetical funding agency receives a proposal, it reads the proposal to determine how the proposal matches the agency's aims, and higher $\theta$ means the proposal aligns with the agency's goals more closely.
elements.sent-2411-470: After learning $\theta$, the agent chooses $y$ resources to save, spends the remaining $x-y$ amount, and derives utility equal to $u(\theta,x-y)$, where $u$ is the agent's instantaneous utility function.
elements.sent-2411-471: The agent chooses $y$ to maximize the sum of current instantaneous utility and expected future utility from any remaining opportunities.
elements.sent-2411-472: Moving forward in time, the agent faces the same problem except with $y$ resources instead of $x$, so the agent's valuation of their resource stock solves a dynamic programming problem with $x$ and $t$ as state variables.

elements.sent-2411-511: Time preferences can be homogeneous with respect to the passage of time in three ways: stationarity, time invariance, and time consistency.
elements.sent-2411-512: We can most easily understand these properties in terms of an evaluation period (the agent's present time) and a consumption period (a known future time for the agent).
elements.sent-2411-513: During the evaluation period, the agent chooses one of two alternatives to consume either upon reaching the consumption period or possibly at a fixed, known amount of time after the consumption period.
elements.sent-2411-514: Stationarity means the agent chooses independently of the consumption period, and time consistency means the agent chooses independently of the evaluation period.
elements.sent-2411-515: Time invariance means that if we change both the evaluation period and the consumption period by the same amount, the agent's choice remains the same.
elements.sent-2411-516: A preference reversal means that an agent makes two different choices depending on the timing of either the decision or the consumption.
elements.sent-2411-517: A static preference reversal is one that results from changing the consumption period (while fixing the evaluation period), and a dynamic preference reversal is the opposite.

elements.sent-2411-700: By performing a variable substitution on each integral, we can rewrite \eq{decomposition as \begin{equation}V_{i+1}'-V_i' =-\sum_{k=0}^{i-1} \int_{\min\{V_{i-k+1}-V_{i-k},\mu(k+1)-\mu(k)\}} ^{\min\{V_{i-k}-V_{i-k-1},\mu(k+1)-\mu(k)\}} \left[1-\zeta^{-1} \left(\frac{y}{\mu(k+1)-\mu(k)}\right)\right]\:dy\nonumber \qquad{}-\int_{\min\{V_1,\mu(i+1)-\mu(i)\}} ^{\mu(i+1)-\mu(i)} \left[1-\zeta^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{\mu(i+1)-\mu(i)}\right)\right]\:dy \label{decomposition-change-var}\end{equation} Suppose $k_1<k_2$.. Then \begin{equation}\Delta\mu(k_1) >\Delta\mu(k_2) \frac y{\Delta\mu(k_1)} <\frac y{\Delta\mu(k_2)} 1-\zeta^{-1}\left(\frac y{\Delta\mu(k_1)}\right) > 1-\zeta^{-1}\left(\frac y{\Delta\mu(k_2)}\right).\end{equation}.}
